external.description: The "Trump J6 fund" refers to a newly announced ~$1.776 billion "Anti-Weaponization Fund" (or similar "weaponization" compensation fund) established by the Trump Justice Department.  Current Status Announcement and origin: As part of settling President Trump's $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS/Treasury over the leak of his tax returns, the DOJ is transferring approximately $1.776 billion (from the Judgment Fund or similar) into this account. This was reported around May 14–19, 2026. Purpose: It creates a systematic process to hear and redress claims of government "weaponization" and "lawfare" under the prior (Biden) administration. A five-member commission (appointed by the Attorney General, with Trump reportedly able to remove members) would review claims, issue apologies, and award monetary relief. Claims processing runs through December 15, 2028. Potential beneficiaries: Reports explicitly note it could include Trump allies investigated or prosecuted under Biden, and some or many of the ~1,600 January 6 defendants (most of whom received pardons/commutations shortly after Trump's 2025 inauguration). Trump has publicly floated compensation for J6 participants who lost jobs/income, and his DOJ has supported refunds of restitution in some cases. Oversight and mechanics: Described as having limited transparency or congressional oversight; critics call the structure highly unusual for taxpayer funds. It is framed by supporters as correcting prior abuses (e.g., selective prosecution).  This is distinct from earlier private fundraising (e.g., via Save America PAC) or general pardons. It uses federal/taxpayer money via executive/ DOJ action tied to the lawsuit settlement.  Pushback and Controversy There has been significant criticism, primarily from Democrats, left-leaning outlets, and government watchdog groups, framing it as a "slush fund," "corruption," or "theft of taxpayer money" to reward allies and J6 participants:  Democratic objections: Figures like Rep. Jamie Raskin, Sen. Ron Wyden, and others have called it an illegal abuse, shakedown, or reward for insurrectionists. Letters and statements demand rejection of payouts to J6 defendants, arguing pardons don't entitle them to refunds or compensation for damages (e.g., to the Capitol, estimated in billions including security/response costs). Media/watchdog coverage: Outlets like NYT, HuffPost, ABC, Rolling Stone, and Public Citizen label it a "vast political slush fund" with minimal oversight, potentially benefiting Trump associates directly. Some tie it to broader J6 restitution refunds (taxpayers absorbing costs after pardons). Republican/Trump-side perspective: Limited public defense in searches, but the administration and supporters argue it addresses documented overreach, two-tiered justice, and ruined lives from Biden-era actions. Trump has distanced himself somewhat from details while calling it "well received" for those "horribly treated." Some earlier J6-related restitution refunds have been pursued via courts.  Broader context on J6 finances: Trump issued broad pardons/commutations for most defendants in early 2025. Taxpayers have already borne repair/security costs from the event (Democrats previously highlighted ~$2.7B estimates). Private pro-Trump PAC/donor money previously supported rally organizers and some defenses, but this new fund shifts to direct government payouts.  This remains a fast-moving, highly partisan issue with ongoing legal/ethical debates. Supporters see it as restorative justice; opponents as unprecedented self-dealing with public money. Details on exact disbursements, eligibility, and any congressional intervention are still developing as of mid-May 2026.
external.title: What is the status of Trump’s J6 fund and where is the pushback on this outright theft of taxpayer money?

Spain, France set benchmark in renewables expansion without biodiversity loss
->pv magazine International | More on "Renewables expansion and biodiversity protection" at BigEarthData.ai | #Renewable #BiodiversityLoss

external.description: Scientists from Switzerland have conducted a comparative analysis of national policies in France, Italy and Spain on environmental integration of renewable energy projects, spatial planning, ecological monitoring and cumulative impact assessment. The study examined how the three countries integrate biodiversity objectives into renewable energy deployment strategies under the European Green Deal framework. The researchers note that France, Italy and Spain are among Southern Europe’s leading renewable energy systems and share high ecological sensitivity, but follow different governance approaches: France operates a centralized model, while Italy and Spain rely on more decentralized, multi-level systems. In the paper “Integrating biodiversity conservation into renewable energy development under the European Green Deal: A comparative analysis of national policies in France, Italy and Spain,” published in Environmental Challenges, the researchers analyzed 46 public policy documents – 15 from France, 13 from Italy, and 18 from Spain – covering energy, biodiversity, the circular economy and cross-cutting policy areas. The aim was to assess how effectively ecological considerations are integrated into energy planning, and how synergies and conflicts between biodiversity and renewable technologies, including solar, wind, bioenergy, hydropower, geothermal and marine energy, are addressed. Spain implementing data-driven spatial planning The study identified Spain as one of the...

In the paper “Integrating biodiversity conservation into renewable energy development under the European Green Deal: A comparative analysis of national policies in France, Italy and Spain,” published in Environmental Challenges, the researchers analyzed 46 public policy documents – 15 from France, 13 from Italy, and 18 from Spain – covering energy, biodiversity, the circular economy and cross-cutting policy areas. The aim was to assess how effectively ecological considerations are integrated into energy planning, and how synergies and conflicts between biodiversity and renewable technologies, including solar, wind, bioenergy, hydropower, geothermal and marine energy, are addressed. record.text: Uninstalling both of those things, best speed increase in Windows really.  Always keep an eye on those two in case Microslop reinstalls them.

Which gets worse if say your internet goes down as then file explorer just sits there going.  "Where's fucking Mycloud."
"I want to use my drives."
"Nope, need to wait for external backup server."
"But..."
"JUST WAIT!"

I'm not joking, want to speed up windows.

Remove copilot and mycloud.  The OS slows down on both of these things being installed, it's like having two copies of Norton installed.

First slows down cpu/gpu
Second fucks with your network speed and local drive access as it has to ping some server 1st

I sincerely believe that MS rollout of Copilot is a the main reason why the public soured over AI more than anything Sam Altman or Dario Amodei has said … as someone who uses AI tools like Claude, I pretty much avoid Copilot unless my brain is pooped and I need to send and email

Just 4% of self-employed workers are putting cash into pensions, with ‘large groups across the UK facing a severe cliff-edge’

Millions of people across Britain are facing a “cliff edge” when they retire due to a chronic shortfall in saving that will require a radical shake-up of the pensions system to fix, a government-backed report has warned.

The Pensions Commission said 15 million people were currently not saving adequately for their retirement, and warned this could rise to as many as 19 million without action. Continue reading...
